reasoning:
The ECB is currently implementing passive Quantitative Tightening (QT), predictably shedding assets to reduce its balance sheet. Bank of America and other analysts forecast the balance sheet to shrink to €6.0 trillion by the end of 2025. FRED data indicates that as of October 24, 2025, total assets were around €6.195 trillion. This current value is temporarily elevated due to traditional early-quarter/quarter-end liquidity operations (it spiked in early October from ~€6.07T to ~€6.20T).

Because of this short-term inflation of the balance sheet in October, it is very likely that as these temporary operations roll off—combined with the structural rundown of bond portfolios—the balance sheet will be distinctly smaller by early November. Thus, for November 2 (0.20), the probability of an increase over late October is quite low. By November 25 (0.25), continued QT will have pushed the structural baseline even lower, though horizon uncertainty mechanically pulls the forecast slightly closer to 0.5. By January 24, 2026 (0.35), year-end or early-year lending could produce seasonal bumps (similar to what was seen in January 2025), but the balance sheet's structural drift strongly suggests it will still settle well below €6.195 trillion. As instructed, estimates are drawn closer to 0.5 for more distant resolution dates to account for greater structural uncertainty and potential policy shifts over longer time horizons.
